summary: Prefer stack allocation over heap allocation in hot paths.
rule: Use POD values, fixed arrays, arrayvec-style bounded scratch, slabs, or prebuilt storage before Box, Vec, String, HashMap, or heap-backed futures in hot paths.
evidence_required: If heap allocation remains, document why ownership or unbounded size is required and measure allocation count or throughput impact.

summary: Prefer bytes internally; convert to strings only at semantic edges.
rule: Hot paths should traffic in &[u8], Bytes, borrowed byte views, or typed POD parsed from bytes. UTF-8 validation and String allocation belong at input/output edges, config surfaces, diagnostics, or APIs whose semantics are truly textual.
evidence_required: Any String or &str in a hot path needs a named semantic reason and a bench or trace showing it is not the throughput limiter.

summary: Borrow or view existing data before instantiating owned data.
rule: Prefer references, borrowed views, slices, cursors, indices, handles, and lifetime-bound parsed structs before allocating owned mirror structures. Owned values are for storage boundaries, async ownership transfer, or APIs that must outlive the source buffer.
evidence_required: Owned mirror structures must state the boundary that requires ownership and include tests for lifetime/ownership behavior.

summary: Zero-copy is best; Copy beats Clone only for small semantic value types.
rule: First try zero-copy borrowed views. If ownership is necessary and the value is small POD with value semantics, make it Copy. Use Clone only when duplication is semantically intentional and cheap enough for the path. Never hide allocation behind clone in a hot path.
evidence_required: Clone in a hot path requires allocation/copy-size evidence or a documented cold-path classification.

summary: Use SIMD-backed search primitives before plain byte loops.
rule: For delimiter, substring, CRLF, varint terminator, mask, and small alternation scans, try memchr, memchr2, memchr3, memchr::memmem, or aho-corasick before writing scalar loops. Hand SIMD is reserved for shapes those crates cannot express and must be benched.
evidence_required: If scalar search remains, document why memchr or aho-corasick does not fit and benchmark representative small, medium, large, and adversarial inputs.

summary: Every performance component needs an explicit allocation budget.
rule: Before landing a performance-sensitive component, state the expected allocations per operation for the hot path, setup path, and cold/error path. The hot path budget should be zero unless the discipline log documents why not.
evidence_required: Bench, allocator instrumentation, criterion allocation arm, or code-level proof through fixed storage and borrowed outputs.

summary: Keep formatting, diagnostics, allocation, and recovery on cold paths.
rule: Hot paths should not build strings, allocate error payloads, format logs, collect Vecs for diagnostics, or perform recovery work. Cold paths may do so if they are explicit and not called from inner loops.
evidence_required: Trace or bench evidence when a diagnostic path is reachable from an inner loop.

summary: Prefer compact data layout over pointer-heavy object graphs.
rule: Use slabs, slot indexes, fixed-cap rings, dense arrays, CSR-like adjacency, small discriminated enums, and cache-padded atomics before HashMap, VecDeque, Arc graphs, trait objects, or nested heap ownership in traversal paths.
evidence_required: If pointer-heavy layout remains in traversal, document the workload and benchmark against a dense or slab-backed alternative.

summary: New PTY or containment behavior composes existing Proxima primitives unless an extension cannot express it.
rule: Before adding a new module, public type, or trait, audit workspace dependencies and existing internal primitives; prefer extending or composing the existing primitive. A discipline row that adds a module must state why no existing primitive can be extended.
evidence_required: List audited primitives and dependencies; cite the existing primitive selected, or document the single-sentence reason a new module is necessary.

summary: A public convenience surface must teach the primitives it composes.
rule: Every new public type or function for PTY or containment work must name and link to its composing primitives, explain why the wrapper exists instead of direct composition, and allow the work to be traced to the OS boundary.
evidence_required: Public doc comments identify primitive links, wrapper purpose, and the relevant syscall, framework, or device boundary.

summary: New core primitives preserve Proxima's no_std plus alloc tier; OS composition remains an explicit std layer.
rule: New tier-1 APIs in `prime`, `proxima-primitives`, or `proxima-runtime` compile with `--no-default-features --features alloc`; std-only facilities are gated and documented. PTY and VM OS boundaries may be std-coupled, but must not push std requirements into core primitives.
evidence_required: For core changes, show alloc-tier compilation; for OS-layer additions, state the std boundary and confirm no core-tier dependency was introduced.

summary: Configurable std-layer components have equivalent config and fluent construction surfaces.
rule: A public configurable std composition type uses conflaguration-derived settings and a fluent builder with equivalent internal state and a Default delegating to the builder; zero- or one-parameter surfaces may use a constructor only when the reason is documented.
evidence_required: Fixture constructs the component through settings and builder and compares state, or documentation establishes that constructor-only shape has at most one configuration parameter.

summary: New readiness sources extend the reactor source model rather than multiplying reactor implementations.
rule: When PTY or containment work needs a source beyond fd readiness, model it as another readiness/source kind alongside fd; do not add a separate reactor merely for pidfd, signal, io_uring, DPDK, SPDK, AF_XDP, or device-queue integration.
evidence_required: Identify the existing reactor/source boundary and document the required source specialization before implementing a new source kind.

summary: When current implementation shape is uncertain, inspect it before proposing or documenting architecture.
rule: Do not write implementation-direction documents from inference; gather cited current-code evidence and use available focused explorer delegation for multiple independent context questions when the environment supports it.
evidence_required: Cite files and symbols read for the decision; where exploration was delegated, retain the bounded questions and returned evidence.

summary: Components land through evidence gates, with negative results retained.
rule: A component discipline row is incomplete with blank evidence cells; performance claims require home-turf benchmark evidence; failed experiments and rollbacks are recorded before the next implementation direction.
evidence_required: Completed discipline row, concrete validation or benchmark log, and recorded failure/rollback when an attempted path did not work.

summary: New primitives state sizing and structural axes before adding generated-profile support.
rule: Treat Prime's generated sizing/profile pattern as reusable infrastructure: for each proposed primitive, state numeric and structural build/runtime axes in the discipline log even when a generalized build-support extraction is not yet implemented.
evidence_required: Discipline row names numeric and structural axes or explicitly states that the component has no varying axes.

summary: Ported wezterm portable-pty code retains attribution and licensing before external release.
rule: PTY-specific code confirmed as a wezterm portable-pty port must carry required attribution and license headers before any external release.
evidence_required: Release audit cites attribution and license headers for transported PTY code.

summary: An opaque hardened-runtime target binary does not load the Proxima DYLD interpose shim used successfully by an owned probe binary.
rule: Do not claim access capture or containment of an opaque hardened-runtime target from DYLD interposition; use it only for owned test children unless the target binary is directly re-verified.
evidence_required: Real-target loader probe: `shim_probe` reports the dylib and returns `proxima-shimmed`; a signed hardened-runtime CLI (exact target binary + version recorded in the external pty-tester discipline log) reports hardened runtime and a `--version` invocation with identical injection variables reports no shim load.

summary: Contained discovery must grant access through a Proxima-owned OS boundary before journaling and later sealing the policy.
rule: Discovery may provide permissive brokered fallthrough only inside a constructed environment with secrets excluded up front; sealing is based on reviewed recorded grants; replay removes ambient host fallback.
evidence_required: Demonstrate the guest/container boundary against the real child, record filesystem and egress grants, then rerun with unmatched resources denied.

summary: A guest VM contains the target binary, but direct shared directories and default NAT do not by themselves create Proxima's recorded resource boundary.
rule: Treat an immutable guest base image as a recorded dependency; route dynamic filesystem inputs and network egress through Proxima-owned observable brokers before claiming discovery, sealing, or replay.
evidence_required: Run the exact macOS target binary in a macOS guest; show filesystem and egress grants emitted by brokered paths; rerun with unmatched grants denied and no ambient fallback.

summary: It is not yet verified whether the opaque hardened-runtime target can operate in a macOS guest with no general NIC and only a Proxima-owned proxy channel.
rule: Do not select HTTP proxy versus packet/gateway mediation until a real guest run measures the target's behavior with explicit proxy configuration and no bypass path.
evidence_required: Guest execution trace using the exact target binary with no general NIC, explicit proxy configuration, and recorded connection attempts.

summary: The Apple Hypervisor scratch backend skipped guest output loads when the host advanced PC after an HVC exit.
rule: On the verified ARM64 Hypervisor.framework HVC output path, resume at the PC reported after the exit; do not apply a host-side instruction increment.
evidence_required: The failed run reported `value=104 pc=0xb8 syndrome=0x5a000000 index=22`; removing the host PC increment made the same signed guest emit `hello from proxima-vm`.

summary: The first Proxima VM experiment is a zero-device bare-metal Pipe ground, with only platform hypervisor leaves.
rule: Represent scratch execution as `ScratchVm: Pipe`; keep guest bytes as input and platform execution in narrow Hypervisor.framework or KVM backends. This proves an owned machine boundary but does not claim an OS guest, target execution, access capture, or replay.
evidence_required: An entitled ARM64 macOS run and an x86_64 `/dev/kvm` run must return bytes emitted by guest trap exits; add observable devices only in experiments that require them.

summary: Every working-system mirror is addressed as a Proxima Pipe; the virtual machine is the internal execution boundary, not the external programming model.
rule: A mirror must expose lifecycle and interactive session calls through `Pipe`, and compose only declared terminal, file, network, credential, and guest-artifact device pipes beneath it. It may atomically replace projected-view or authority routes behind the stable mirror interface for discovery, sealing, replay, masking, or denial, but route replacement must never introduce an undeclared ambient host escape. Because ordinary swaps preserve in-flight calls, immediate revocation requires a quiesce, pause, or stop operation that invalidates active access before replacement.
evidence_required: Boot a macOS guest through a mirror pipe; enter a guest terminal session; route one declared workspace input and one declared network path through recordable inner pipes; atomically replace a visible resource with masked or deny behavior; demonstrate the stable session observes the changed future view; separately stop or quiesce a mirror to prove immediate revocation does not allow an in-flight old-route completion.

summary: The libc-interpose shim and proxima-vm must present the same ChildRequest/ChildResponse dispatch surface; adding a request variant to one without the other is a parity regression.
rule: `proxima_process::protocol::{ChildRequest, ChildResponse}` is the shared dispatch contract. Both containment mechanisms (libc-shim for owned children; proxima-vm for hardened-runtime / opaque binaries) MUST consume identical variants so a single DispatchChoice config or typed chain routes through either. proxima-vm will be a strict superset (it can intercept syscalls the libc-shim cannot — e.g. on a signed hardened-runtime target), but for every variant the libc-shim handles, proxima-vm MUST also handle it. Configure-once, route-through-either-mechanism is the design invariant.
evidence_required: A parity test exists at the workspace level (or per-protocol-variant) that asserts: for the same DispatchChoice config, the libc-shim's intercepted call and the proxima-vm's intercepted call produce equivalent ChildResponse payloads against the same dispatch chain. When the test does not yet exist, the discipline log C8c row tracks the gap and blocks landing new ChildRequest variants until both consumers are wired.

summary: QUIC + H3 rewrite: proxima-quic (facade crate) + proxima-http::http3 (facade, folded from the former proxima-h3 crate) + proxima-protocols::quic + proxima-protocols::http3_codec (sans-IO proto modules, folded from the former proxima-quic-proto + proxima-h3-proto crates). Proto crates are tier-1 sans-IO, runtime-agnostic, publicly consumable. Facade crates are tier-2 runtime-agnostic with no tokio in production paths.
rule: All QUIC + H3 work in this rewrite lands with a strict dependency direction: proxima-http::http3 -> proxima-quic -> proxima-protocols::quic; proxima-http::http3 -> proxima-protocols::http3_codec -> proxima-protocols::quic. Sans-IO proto modules have ZERO dependencies on proxima-quic, proxima-http, prime, tokio, std::net. Facade crates depend on prime as default driver; tokio compat is feature-gated and off by default. Sans-IO proto crates are versioned for external consumption.
evidence_required: proxima-protocols (quic + http3_codec modules) compiles under --no-default-features --features alloc on host and thumbv7m-none-eabi targets. proxima-quic + proxima-http production builds (after C29 lands the facade rewrite, post-C41 cutover) produce zero transitive tokio symbols verified by `cargo tree -i tokio` returning empty.

summary: Sans-IO components in proxima-protocols::quic + proxima-protocols::http3_codec bind on the six-clause sans-IO contract from guiding-principles.md principle 11. The 0-hot-alloc clause (clause 2) is binding for tier-3-promotable modules; tier-1 modules whose state genuinely scales unbounded with peer behaviour may file a documented exception via alloc-budget.md, but each exception MUST list the per-operation alloc count and the planned redesign that would re-eliminate it.
rule: No sans-IO component lands without ALL six clauses marked DONE with evidence in its discipline.md row OR explicit exception language for the clauses that don't fully bind. The clauses are: (1) discriminated enum FSM or typestate (no Box<dyn>, no Arc<Mutex<State>>) — binding always; (2) **0 hot-path allocs** verified by tracking_allocator over 100k iterations — binding for tier-3-promotable modules and for the inner-loop of every tier-1 module; tier-1 modules whose lifetime-of-event payload genuinely outlives the inbound borrow (e.g. H3 server/client event queues, QPACK decoded fields) MUST file a row in docs/proxima-quic/alloc-budget.md naming the alloc site, the per-operation cost, and the redesign that would remove it (DC-H3-QPACK-DECODE-OWNS-VECS and DC-H3-FACADE-EVENTS-OWN are the seed entries); (3) bench arms across multiple input sizes + multi-arch SIMD + adversarial inputs + named home-turf incumbent arm + CoV-tracked + alloc count per arm + iai-callgrind cycle counts — binding always; (4) hot-path performance gates from the workspace AGENTS.md bind (500 MB / >=55 MB/s / sub-1ms / zero-copy / 0-alloc / lock-free reads / no O(N^2)) — sub-1ms applies per documented payload-shape (e.g. for SETTINGS apply_payload the within-credit shape MUST be sub-1ms; the beyond-credit shape MUST stay sub-quadratic + be unreachable through the listener's advertised credit); (5) real-world test data (rcgen certs, captured QUIC handshakes from Cloudflare/Google/quiche/s2n-quic/qlog) — binding always; (6) compile-time correctness everywhere mechanically possible (exhaustive match, per-variant data ownership, const-generic caps, #[must_use], #[non_exhaustive], newtype IDs) — binding always.
evidence_required: Each row in the component discipline log marks all six clauses DONE with the specific evidence inline. Clauses that do not bind for the row's tier carry an explicit pointer to the alloc-budget.md exception entry or the perf-targets.md per-payload-shape carve-out. Failures of binding clauses must be rolled back per the disciplined-component skill, not papered over.

summary: TLS 1.3 for proxima-protocols::quic lives at tier-1 (no_std + alloc) via rustls in no_std + alloc mode with the aws-lc-rs crypto provider; inline TLS state machine is a spike-only fallback if upstream rustls gaps prove too large.
rule: The TLS 1.3 handshake (C10) is built on rustls::quic::Connection using rustls::crypto::aws_lc_rs::default_provider(), compiled with rustls's no_std + alloc feature subset. If A0 / C10 spike finds rustls's no_std + alloc support insufficient, the first response is an upstream PR to rustls. Only when upstream gaps are unfixable in scope does the inline TLS fallback (tls-inline feature) fire — and then with mandatory /security-audit at every stage. The std-tier proxima-quic facade does NOT have a separate TLS path; TLS lives in the proto crate.
evidence_required: The A3 row (C10 TLS sans-IO) in the component discipline log records the spike outcome (green / small gaps PR'd upstream / large gaps fallback to tls-inline) with the rustls + aws-lc-rs version pinned, plus a /security-audit pass for any inline code.

summary: aws-lc-rs is the default crypto provider for proxima-protocols::quic (and for TLS via rustls). an external crypto crate composes aws-lc-rs already (ChaCha20-Poly1305, AES-GCM); we reuse that for QUIC packet protection.
rule: All AEAD (ChaCha20-Poly1305, AES-128-GCM, AES-256-GCM), HKDF (SHA-256, SHA-384), digest (SHA-256), and X25519 used by proxima-protocols::quic come from aws-lc-rs via either direct use or via composition with an external crypto crate. Other crypto providers (ring, BoringSSL bindings, RustCrypto pure) MAY land later as opt-in features but are not the default. The proto crate's Cargo.toml workspace dep is `aws-lc-rs = { workspace = true, default-features = false }` to keep the no_std + alloc path open. FIPS is std-only in aws-lc-rs and is NOT enabled in the default build.
evidence_required: proxima-protocols (quic module) with --no-default-features --features alloc,quic compiles and the AEAD round-trips a known-good test vector (RFC 8439 ChaCha20-Poly1305 test vector + RFC 9001 §A test vector for QUIC initial keys). the external crypto crate's AEAD composition verified in C6 row.

summary: quic_impl=native is the runtime-agnostic native facade; it builds today with no quinn in its dep tree (verified by the always-on quic-h3-gate cell). tokio remains a direct dependency of the native facade today because the UDP accept loop uses tokio::net + tokio::spawn — that is the present production reality and is explicitly NOT a tokio-free build.
rule: proxima-build's Profile validation: when quic_impl=native, no executor constraint is imposed at the profile-axis level. proxima-http::http3 native + proxima-quic native BOTH depend on tokio today (gated on the parent native / quinn-compat feature); a future swap onto prime's Datagram reactor source is tracked separately and is NOT a precondition for the dual-surface decision. The TOKIO_FREE_FACADE_ENFORCE gate cell in scripts/quic-h3-gate.sh is staged but stays off by default; it cannot pass until both (a) prime gets a Datagram reactor source and (b) prime's own std feature is split (the 'Tokio transitive leak via prime's std feature (C31)' edge in the component edge log). h3_impl=native requires quic_impl=native (the facades are paired); h3_impl=quinn requires quic_impl=quinn for the legacy bridge.
evidence_required: Tests in proxima-build/src/profile.rs: `quic_impl_native_accepts_executor_prime`, `quic_impl_quinn_rejects_executor_prime`, `h3_impl_native_requires_quic_impl_native`, `h3_impl_h3_quinn_requires_quic_impl_quinn`, `quic_disabled_forces_quic_impl_none`. Plus the always-on `proxima-http quinn-free (native feature only)` cell in scripts/quic-h3-gate.sh that runs `cargo tree -p proxima-http --no-default-features --features http3-native -i quinn` and asserts empty.

summary: Sans-IO leaf modules in proxima-protocols::quic + proxima-protocols::http3_codec target tier-3 (bare no_std + no alloc) promotion by default. Tier-1 (no_std + alloc) is the fallback only when state genuinely scales unbounded with peer behavior. Goal: >=60% of proto-crate module count compiles at tier-3.
rule: Every component row in the component discipline log records tier-3 status as PROMOTED, EXCLUDED, or DEFERRED with one-line justification. Promotion-eligible modules: varint, packet::header, frame parse, transport_parameters, crypto::expand_label, crypto::aead (in-place), crypto::header_protection, connection_id, packet_number, range_set, QPACK static-table, QPACK varint. Exclusion-eligible modules: tls::Connection (rustls owns alloc), connection state-machine (retransmit queue scales unbounded), endpoint demux table, QPACK dynamic-table, streams (per-stream buffers), congestion sent-packets queue. All eligibility decisions in the row.
evidence_required: proxima-protocols (quic + http3_codec modules) compiles under --no-default-features --features no-alloc on host AND thumbv7m-none-eabi targets at every component landing. quic-h3-gate.sh verifies.

summary: proxima-quic + proxima-h3 ship BOTH the native facade (over the sans-IO proto crates) AND the quinn-compat facade indefinitely. There is no atomic-cutover commit that deletes quinn from the workspace. Consumers choose per-build via the `native` vs `quinn-compat` Cargo feature on each facade crate. Supersedes the prior `proxima.decision.quic_h3_cutover_supersedes_quinn` invariant.
rule: Both feature surfaces are first-class and tested. Default features for both crates remain `["quinn-compat", "native"]` (each consumer narrows as needed). Workspace dep on proxima-quic carries `default-features = false` so a downstream `--features native` build does NOT silently pull quinn through proxima-quic's defaults. The CI gate `proxima-http quinn-free (native feature only)` enforces that the native path stays quinn-free. quinn-compat stays available because: (a) external consumers of the quinn ecosystem are entitled to keep using it; (b) the native facade does not yet implement every quinn feature (multipath, 0-RTT under load, etc.) — the legacy bridge is the escape hatch until native reaches parity. A future deprecation, if it happens, ships its own new invariant.
evidence_required: proxima-http/Cargo.toml has both `http3-native` and `http3-quinn-compat` features; the workspace proxima-quic entry has `default-features = false`; scripts/quic-h3-gate.sh contains the always-on `proxima-http quinn-free (native feature only)` cell verifying `cargo tree -p proxima-http --no-default-features --features http3-native -i quinn` reports no matching packages.

summary: Alert events ride ProtocolEvent::Custom; no new top-level event type.
rule: AlertEvent serializes into ProtocolEvent::Custom { kind: "alert", payload } for persistence/replay; in-process Pipes carry it via Body::typed(AlertEvent). No new ProtocolEvent variant.
evidence_required: proxima-recording/src/event.rs:160
proxima-primitives/src/pipe/body.rs:199

summary: All proxima-notify (proxima-patterns::alert) sinks, sources, and dispatchers are Pipe impls.
rule: No new trait peers to Pipe. Routing/dedup/fanout/retry/timeout come from existing middleware (RoutingPipe, Tee, SharedRingTee, Retry, Isolate, SwappablePipe).
evidence_required: proxima-primitives/src/pipe/primitives.rs:89

summary: The proxima-patterns::alert::event module (formerly proxima-notify::event) uses typed builders + serde only; no conflaguration::Settings derive (P4 layering caveat).
rule: proxima-patterns::alert::event (formerly proxima-notify::event, and before that the proxima-notify-proto crate, folded in at b70f8b4) carries bounded data types with bon::Builder + serde::Serialize/Deserialize. Settings-backed configuration lives on the std facade Pipes (scheduled_trigger, stdout_alert, stdio_guidance, webhook_out) only, not in the event module.
evidence_required: guiding-principles principle 4 layering caveat

summary: Durable delivery is at-least-once; exactly-once requires per-sink idempotence proof.
rule: BinSink + OffsetCursor + DurableConsumerPipe provide at-least-once. Exactly-once delivery is not claimed by any component without a separate behavioral contract on the consuming sink (e.g. content-addressed dedup) that the sink discipline-log row argues explicitly.
evidence_required: the component discipline log C7 and C8 rows

summary: ScheduledTriggerPipe is NOT IdempotentSideEffectFree even if its inner is.
rule: ScheduledTriggerPipe reads the clock; fired_at varies per call; observationally NOT identical across repeated dispatches. The marker is impl-ed only on types whose behavioral contract is enforceable locally.
evidence_required: proxima-core/src/markers.rs IdempotentSideEffectFree definition

summary: Per-protocol integrations (Telegram, Discord, PagerDuty, ntfy, etc.) are TOML compositions of existing primitives, not new Pipes or crates.
rule: Each integration = HttpUpstream + Transform (body_template DSL) + Validate (proxima-config::schema) + Retry + Isolate composed in a [pipes.<name>] TOML entry. URL embeds `${env:TOKEN}`-style placeholders. Sim mode flips one URL line to a local listener + RoutingPipe + SynthUpstream that emits per-route response shapes. If sim works, real works (provable via proxima-config::schema validation of both request and response shapes). Adding a new integration = adding TOML; never adding Rust.
evidence_required: docs/proxima-notify/SUBSTRATE.md `Architectural consolidation (post-2ad062c push)` section; docs/proxima-notify/examples/*.toml configs (lands in proxima-template-dsl-and-sim follow-up)

summary: Do not embed upstream pgwire's server API in Proxima's prime/native path: pgwire 0.28 server-api pulls tokio/tokio-util/tokio-rustls and owns a tokio Framed connection loop.
rule: PostgreSQL wire support must start with a Proxima-owned sans-IO codec/session layer and a separate stream facade. Upstream pgwire remains an incumbent comparison and interoperability oracle, not a production dependency for the prime/native surface.
evidence_required: `cargo tree -i tokio` against a consumer crate embedding pgwire's server-api feature shows tokio pulled in via pgwire, tokio-rustls, and tokio-util; the component discipline log records the source files inspected.

summary: The PostgreSQL wire codec target is an explicit FSM with a no_std + no_alloc leaf message layer; session no-alloc requires caller-supplied bounded storage.
rule: `proxima-protocols::pgwire_codec` (folded from the former proxima-pgwire-codec crate) should parse frontend messages as borrowed views and encode backend messages into caller-owned output storage. The frame/message FSM must not require std, heap allocation, tokio, or upstream pgwire. The connection/session FSM may remain no-alloc only when statement, portal, auth, and output buffers are provided by the caller as bounded storage; ergonomic allocation belongs in the std facade.
evidence_required: the component discipline log `FSM and tier direction` plus no-alloc build gate for `proxima-protocols::pgwire_codec`.

summary: Pgwire over TCP reuses the existing proxima-tls stream substrate; TLS is not new codec work.
rule: `proxima-protocols::pgwire_codec` must model PostgreSQL SSLRequest as a frontend protocol transition but must not implement TLS. `proxima-pgwire` should use `proxima-tls::build_acceptor_futures_io` for prime/proxima-primitives::stream accepted connections and may reuse tokio-rustls only in legacy listener paths that already require tokio compatibility. QUIC/H3 TLS evidence lives in proxima-protocols::quic and is separate from the TCP stream TLS path.
evidence_required: the component discipline log `Existing TLS substrate`; proxima-tls/src/imp.rs defines `build_acceptor` and `build_acceptor_futures_io`; proxima-tls/src/connector.rs defines `TlsStreamUpstream`; proxima-listen/src/handle.rs serializes listener TLS config into specs.

summary: PostgreSQL wire support targets full protocol capability; implementation phases are sequencing only, not a reduced product boundary.
rule: `proxima-protocols::pgwire_codec` and `proxima-pgwire` must be planned against the full PostgreSQL protocol matrix: startup negotiation, SSL/GSS/cancel requests, all documented frontend/backend messages for the selected protocol version, full auth message flow including SASL/SCRAM, simple query, extended query, prepared statements, portals, COPY in/out/both, text/binary format codes, error recovery, transaction status, and real-client compatibility. Any deferred item must have a named gate in the component discipline log and must not be described as out of scope.
evidence_required: the component discipline log `Full capability target`; a full capability matrix before implementation is considered complete; real-client fixtures for startup/simple/extended/copy/cancel/auth flows.

summary: SQLx is a runtime-bound compatibility client; ORMs are optional downstream canaries, not design drivers.
rule: SQLx compatibility must be tested over the wire from an external harness because SQLx selects an executor through runtime feature flags instead of exposing a runtime-neutral futures-IO substrate. SQLx must not enter `proxima-protocols::pgwire_codec`, `proxima-pgwire`, or the prime/native production dependency graph; it is a black-box client compatibility test only. ORMs such as SeaORM are optional downstream canaries only: full pgwire capability should make them possible as ordinary PostgreSQL clients, but ORM behavior must not drive the codec shape, query-handler contract, or acceptance criteria. This does not weaken Proxima's runtime-agnostic server contract: pgwire must compose `proxima-primitives::stream` (`futures::io`) and `ServeContext`'s runtime-matched acceptor factory.
evidence_required: the component discipline log `Client compatibility harnesses`; proxima-primitives/src/stream/mod.rs says the byte-stream primitive is futures::io, not tokio::io; proxima-listen/src/lib.rs exposes runtime and acceptor_factory in ServeContext; SQLx feature metadata lists runtime feature flags.

summary: proxima-protocols::pgwire_codec (folded from the former proxima-pgwire-codec crate) stays bare no_std + no-alloc.
rule: The codec crate must keep compiling for thumbv7em-none-eabihf with no alloc: decode stays borrowed-view (validated-once lazy iterators), encode stays caller-owned buffers, session FSM stays POD. Anything needing the heap belongs in proxima-pgwire (std facade).
evidence_required: cargo build -p proxima-protocols --no-default-features --features pgwire_codec --target thumbv7em-none-eabihf
tests/alloc_counter.rs zero-alloc proof stays green

summary: proxima-pgwire core stays tokio-free without default features.
rule: cargo tree -p proxima-pgwire --no-default-features -e normal -i tokio must stay empty; tokio enters only via the listen feature graph (proxima-listen) and the off-by-default tokio-compat feature.
evidence_required: cargo tree -p proxima-pgwire --no-default-features -e normal -i tokio prints nothing

summary: Upstream pgwire 0.28 is the golden oracle, with one documented bug.
rule: Encode/decode changes must keep golden parity with upstream pgwire 0.28 messages except CopyFail, where upstream emits tag 'c' (constant misreference) and the spec requires 'f'; our codec follows the spec per P14 proof in tests/golden_pgwire.rs.
evidence_required: tests/golden_pgwire.rs green

summary: proxima-storage::pmem's crash-consistency update FSM (folded from the former proxima-pmem crate) is copy-on-write with an atomic 8-byte root swap (double-buffer / ping-pong), selected over undo- and redo-logging via /algorithm-rigor (unanimous Borda 6/3/0 + critic) because it yields the simplest PROVABLE persistence-reordering crash oracle.
rule: The proxima-storage::pmem leaf is sans-IO tier-3 (no_std + no-alloc), pure Rust zero C linked; PMDK/libpmem is a parity reference, not a dependency. Crash consistency uses CoW + atomic 8-byte root swap: write the new value into the dead of two slots, persist it (B1), flip a single 8-byte aligned root word, persist the root (B2). recover() reads ONLY the 8-byte ADR-atomic root and returns the live slot — no log, no replay — so the reordering oracle's search space is O(1) in payload size (dead-slot partial persistence is invisible; the root's crash subset is {old,new}, never torn). Slots may be arbitrary length; only the root needs 8-byte atomicity; the single load-bearing barrier is B1-before-root-flip. The exhaustive pmreorder-analog oracle (every crash subset -> old-or-new) is the correctness gate and lives in the crate's tests; it must catch a missing-B1 bug. Persistence primitives flush/drain/persist live in persist.rs (x86_64 clwb/clflushopt/clflush+sfence, aarch64-linux DC CVAC+DSB, documented dev no-op fallback) and are the only unsafe (the hardware boundary). DAX mmap std facade, a consumer crate's cdb-on-pmem integration, and the CXL hardware target are out of scope / separate components.
evidence_required: the component discipline log C1 row + algorithm-rigor resolution; proxima-storage builds default + --no-default-features --features pmem + thumbv7m-none-eabi + x86_64-linux; 21 nextest incl. exhaustive oracle + missing-B1 regression + 3 proptest props; clippy -D warnings clean; ZERO C linked incl. the bench (incumbent is pure-Rust File::sync_data, no libc); real x86 durable arm measured on host-b (i7-9700K clflushopt+sfence) = crash-consistent commit 154 ns vs fsync 824 ns = 5.4x cheaper on instruction cost (durability parity needs pmem silicon).

summary: Box-free by default across the whole workspace, not only sans-IO/proto (guiding-principles principle 20).
rule: Do not use Box<dyn Trait>, Box<dyn Future>, Box::pin, or #[async_trait] (it boxes) unless genuinely unavoidable. Prefer a discriminated enum + exhaustive match for closed-set runtime polymorphism, typestate or consume-old-produce-new enums for state machines, generic type parameters for caller-supplied strategies, and a state-machine future or impl Future return for fixed-type futures. Legitimate Box (allowed, no justification-tax): heterogeneous dispatch over an OPEN/unbounded set the compiler cannot know (plugin registry, user-extensible I/O-facade Box<dyn Pipe> layer), recursive types needing indirection, and shrinking a MEASURED large enum variant that bloats the common path — each such Box in new code carries a one-line why. A Box on a hot path is a representation-shortcut regression until a discipline-log note proves otherwise.
evidence_required: Any Box<dyn>/Box::pin/#[async_trait] in new code carries a one-line why naming which legitimate-Box category it falls under; hot-path Box carries measured justification in the discipline row.

summary: RPITIT (return-position impl Trait in trait) for async/impl-returning trait methods; never #[async_trait] or hand-boxed futures (guiding-principles principle 20).
rule: Async and impl-returning trait methods use return-position impl Trait in trait — fn f(&self) -> impl Future<Output=..> or async fn in trait (edition 2024) — NOT #[async_trait] and NOT -> Pin<Box<dyn Future>>. RPITIT is the stable, allocation-free, monomorphised, box-free shape and keeps the future type concrete for the caller. The poll-based poll_*(&self, cx) -> Poll<..> shape (proxima_net::PacketListener; quic axiom G facade) is the other acceptable box-free async surface, preferred where the caller drives readiness (reactor, cancellation). A trait using RPITIT is not dyn-compatible by design, consistent with the box-free default; a genuinely-open set forcing dyn is the measured-exception Box case.
evidence_required: New async trait methods are RPITIT or poll-based; no async_trait dependency and no Pin<Box<dyn Future>> return in new trait definitions.

summary: Blocking is a last resort; exhaust lock-free and async-yielding options before a synchronous mutex.
rule: Order, strongest to weakest: (1) lock-free (single-writer/ownership, atomics, arc-swap snapshot, ArrayQueue+AtomicWaker); (2) in async context, serialize with an ASYNC gate -- a waker-based async lock or a single-consumer pipe/gate that YIELDS the task to the executor instead of parking a thread; pure atomics+wakers, no OS futex, so it works at EVERY tier including bare metal (prime's executor drives the wakers). A single-consumer pipe IS serialized access -- an async mutex in disguise; (3) a SYNCHRONOUS blocking mutex only when you must park a real thread OUTSIDE async (sync FFI boundary, dedicated blocking worker), and then ALWAYS the canonical `proxima_primitives::sync::Mutex` (folded from the former proxima-lock crate), never a bare `parking_lot::Mutex`/`std::sync::Mutex` dropped in directly. proxima-primitives::sync is tier-resolved: parking_lot on std (a passthrough, std unchanged), an `atomic-wait` futex raw mutex on no_std+OS; (4) a spinlock is ruled out. Physics: a sync blocking lock parks a thread, needs an OS futex, reaches only no_std+OS, never bare metal. An async lock parks nothing, so it reaches every tier. Bare-metal control-plane must be lock-free or async, never a sync mutex.
evidence_required: Any synchronous blocking mutex must be `proxima_primitives::sync::Mutex`, never a bare `parking_lot::Mutex` or `std::sync::Mutex`; a code review or grep showing bare usage is a violation.
A `parking_lot`/`std::sync::Mutex` reachable only from a std-gated module must be behind an optional, std-gated dependency so the no_std build never compiles it.
A spinlock choice must be replaced with the async gate or proxima_primitives::sync::Mutex alternative before landing.

summary: proxima builds its OWN poll-based AsyncRead/AsyncWrite over an associated Error type (never std::io::Error) rather than adopting futures::io / tokio::io / embedded-io-async, so the async IO seam compiles at all four tiers {no_std,std}x{no_alloc,alloc} including bare metal.
rule: futures::io::AsyncRead/AsyncWrite and tokio::io are std-only: their trait signatures are defined over std::io::Error, so any adapter over them (proxy_protocol::Prepended, read_header, the h1/h2/h3/quic/pgwire listeners, preface-sniff) inherits std and cannot compile at the no_std/no_alloc floor. embedded-io-async is no_std but uses async-fn-in-trait, which hides the state machine and is rejected under guiding-principle 11 (explicit poll-based state machines, no Box). RISC (P1): proxima defines a minimal poll-based seam in proxima-core behind the default-off io-async feature -- AsyncRead{type Error; poll_read(Pin<&mut Self>,&mut Context,&mut [u8])->Poll<Result<usize,Error>>} and AsyncWrite{poll_write/poll_flush/poll_close} -- zero new deps, only core::pin+core::task+an associated Error (P3: never std::io::Error). It monomorphises to the same machine code as futures::io (a trait is not faster), so the differentiator is tier-reach, proven by the 4-corner build matrix, not a throughput number. A std bridge (blanket impls over futures::io/tokio with Error=std::io::Error) is a separate follow-on component; the IO adapters then lift onto this seam instead of std-gating. ADDENDUM (2026-07-16, concentrate-async-read-write, the component edge log): this seam earns PUBLIC existence ONLY at the no_std/no-alloc floor (RingSource/RingSink are the concrete proof) -- a type that is ALREADY std-only (a real socket, e.g. PrimeTcpConnection wrapping prime::os::net::TcpStream) must implement futures::io and STOP there, never carry a second, redundant impl of this seam too. A prior pass added exactly that redundant impl as a 'proof the seam reaches a real socket' exercise; it had zero consumers outside its own tests and was the direct cause of a multi-hour over-build in a later session (an agent could not tell which AsyncRead was canonical for a real socket and reinvented plumbing instead of using the already-working futures::io path). Removed. The rule going forward: futures::io is the industry-standard, std-tier canonical trait everywhere std is available (verified: prime::os::net::TcpStream implements it, proxima_primitives::stream::StreamConnection requires it, serve_h1_connection binds it); this seam is the fallback ONLY where futures::io cannot compile at all.
evidence_required: the component discipline log C1 row with the 4-corner build matrix filled; proxima-core builds --features io-async at std+alloc, --no-default-features --features alloc,io-async, --no-default-features --features io-async (no_std+no_alloc), and the same on thumbv7m-none-eabi; clippy -D warnings clean at the no_std+no_alloc corner; futures::io is x at every no_std corner by construction (defined over std::io::Error).
